channel.title: Php on ErrorCode — Error Code Reference & Solutions
channel.description: Recent content in Php on ErrorCode — Error Code Reference & Solutions

item.title: [Solution] Composer Out of Memory Fix
item.description:
Composer Out of Memory

Fix Composer out of memory errors. Handle PHP memory limit, large dependency trees, and optimization..

What This Error Means

Common error scenarios include:

  • Connection or network failures
  • Invalid configuration or options
  • Resource not found or unavailable
  • Permission or access denied

Common Causes

<?php
// Cause 1: Incorrect configuration or missing setup
// Cause 2: Network or connection issues
// Cause 3: Invalid input or parameters
// Cause 4: Missing dependencies or resources
?>

How to Fix

Fix 1: Verify configuration and setup

<?php
// Check configuration values and ensure required setup
// Verify the package/library is properly configured
?>

Fix 2: Add proper error handling

<?php
try {
 // Use the package/library with proper error handling
} catch (\Exception $e) {
 error_log('Error: ' . $e->getMessage());
 // Handle gracefully
}
?>

Fix 3: Validate input and add checks

<?php
// Validate input before processing
// Check existence before accessing resources
// Use type declarations and strict comparisons
?>

Examples

<?php
// Common error handling pattern
try {
 $result = doSomething();
 echo $result;
} catch (\Exception $e) {
 error_log('Error: ' . $e->getMessage());
 http_response_code(500);
 echo json_encode(['error' => $e->getMessage()]);
}
?>

Related Errors

  • PHP Fatal Error — fatal error
  • PHP Warning — warning
  • PHP Notice — notice

item.title: [Solution] Composer Platform Error Fix
item.description:
Composer Platform Error

Fix Composer platform requirement errors. Handle PHP version, extension, and platform config issues..

What This Error Means

Common error scenarios include:

  • Connection or network failures
  • Invalid configuration or options
  • Resource not found or unavailable
  • Permission or access denied

Common Causes

// Verify the package/library is properly configured

 // Use the package/library with proper error handling
} catch (\Exception $e) {

 // Handle gracefully

<?php

// Check existence before accessing resources
// Use type declarations and strict comparisons
?>

Examples

 echo $result;
} catch (\Exception $e) {
 error_log('Error: ' . $e->getMessage());
 http_response_code(500);

}
?>

  • PHP Fatal Error — fatal error
  • PHP Warning — warning
  • PHP Notice — notice

PHP Deprecated Function Usage

// Cause 2: Network or connection issues

// Verify the package/library is properly configured
?>

Fix 2: Add proper error handling

<?php
try {
 // Use the package/library with proper error handling
} catch (\Exception $e) {
 error_log('Error: ' . $e->getMessage());
 // Handle gracefully
}

Fix 3: Validate input and add checks

<?php
// Validate input before processing
// Check existence before accessing resources
// Use type declarations and strict comparisons
?>

Examples

<?php
// Common error handling pattern

 echo $result;

 echo json_encode(['error' => $e->getMessage()]);
}
?>

  • PHP Fatal Error — fatal error
  • PHP Warning — warning
  • PHP Notice — notice

item.title: [Solution] PHP Deprecated Function Usage Warning Fix
item.description:
PHP Deprecated Function Usage Warning Fix

A PHP deprecated warning is triggered when you call a function that has been marked as deprecated. The function still works but may be removed in a future version.

What This Error Means

PHP marks functions as deprecated when a newer, better alternative exists. The deprecated function will continue to work for now, but calling it emits an E_DEPRECATED notice. In PHP 8.x, some deprecated features trigger E_USER_DEPRECATED or fatal errors.

item.title: [Solution] PHP DNF Type Error Fix
item.description:
PHP DNF Type Error

Fix DNF (Disjunctive Normal Form) type errors in PHP 8.2+. Handle intersection and union type combinations..

What This Error Means

Common error scenarios include:

  • Connection or network failures
  • Invalid configuration or options
  • Resource not found or unavailable
  • Permission or access denied

Common Causes

<?php
// Cause 1: Incorrect configuration or missing setup
// Cause 2: Network or connection issues
// Cause 3: Invalid input or parameters
// Cause 4: Missing dependencies or resources
?>

How to Fix

Fix 1: Verify configuration and setup

<?php
// Check configuration values and ensure required setup
// Verify the package/library is properly configured
?>

Fix 2: Add proper error handling

<?php
try {
 // Use the package/library with proper error handling
} catch (\Exception $e) {
 error_log('Error: ' . $e->getMessage());
 // Handle gracefully
}
?>

Fix 3: Validate input and add checks

<?php
// Validate input before processing
// Check existence before accessing resources
// Use type declarations and strict comparisons
?>

Examples

<?php
// Common error handling pattern
try {
 $result = doSomething();
 echo $result;
} catch (\Exception $e) {
 error_log('Error: ' . $e->getMessage());
 http_response_code(500);
 echo json_encode(['error' => $e->getMessage()]);
}
?>

Related Errors

  • PHP Fatal Error — fatal error
  • PHP Warning — warning
  • PHP Notice — notice

item.title: [Solution] PHP DNF Type: Syntax Error Fix
item.description:
PHP DNF Type: Syntax Error Fix

A PHP DNF type error occurs when you use incorrect syntax for Disjunctive Normal Form types. PHP 8.2 allows combining intersection types with union types in function parameters.

What This Error Means

PHP 8.2 introduced Disjunctive Normal Form (DNF) types, which allow combining intersection types (A&B) with union types (A|B) in parentheses. For example, (A&B)|null means “an object that implements both A and B, or null.”

item.title: [Solution] PHP Dynamic Property Deprecated Error Fix
item.description:
PHP Dynamic Property Deprecated Error Fix

A PHP dynamic property error occurs when you create properties on an object that are not declared in the class. This has been deprecated in PHP 8.2 and will become an error in PHP 9.0.

What This Error Means

PHP 8.2 deprecated the creation of dynamic properties — assigning a value to a property that doesn’t exist in the class definition. This was previously allowed via __set() magic method or implicit creation. Future PHP versions will throw an error instead of a deprecation warning.

item.title: [Solution] PHP Dynamic Property Error Fix
item.description:
PHP Dynamic Property Error

Fix dynamic property deprecation errors in PHP 8.2+. Handle __get/__set, #[AllowDynamicProperties], and migration..

}
?>

  • PHP Warning — warning
  • PHP Notice — notice

item.title: [Solution] PHP Enum Case Error Fix
item.description:
PHP Enum Case Error

Fix Enum case errors in PHP 8.1+. Handle backed enums, unit enums, and enum method usage..

What This Error Means

Common error scenarios include:

  • Connection or network failures
  • Invalid configuration or options
  • Resource not found or unavailable
  • Permission or access denied

Common Causes

<?php
// Cause 1: Incorrect configuration or missing setup
// Cause 2: Network or connection issues
// Cause 3: Invalid input or parameters
// Cause 4: Missing dependencies or resources
?>

How to Fix

Fix 1: Verify configuration and setup

<?php
// Check configuration values and ensure required setup
// Verify the package/library is properly configured
?>

Fix 2: Add proper error handling

<?php
try {
 // Use the package/library with proper error handling
} catch (\Exception $e) {
 error_log('Error: ' . $e->getMessage());
 // Handle gracefully
}
?>

Fix 3: Validate input and add checks

<?php
// Validate input before processing
// Check existence before accessing resources
// Use type declarations and strict comparisons
?>

Examples

<?php
// Common error handling pattern
try {
 $result = doSomething();
 echo $result;
} catch (\Exception $e) {
 error_log('Error: ' . $e->getMessage());
 http_response_code(500);
 echo json_encode(['error' => $e->getMessage()]);
}
?>

Related Errors

  • PHP Fatal Error — fatal error
  • PHP Warning — warning
  • PHP Notice — notice

item.title: [Solution] PHP Enum Case Not Handled Error Fix
item.description:
PHP Enum: Case Not Handled Error Fix

A PHP Enum error occurs when a match expression or switch statement does not handle all possible enum cases. PHP enums are strict — you must handle every case or use a default.

What This Error Means

PHP 8.1 introduced backed and non-backed enums. When using enums with match, PHP expects exhaustive handling of all cases unless a default branch is provided. Missing a case produces a runtime error when that unhandled case is encountered.

item.title: [Solution] PHP Fiber Error Fix
item.description:
PHP Fiber Error

Fix Fiber errors in PHP 8.1+. Handle fiber creation, suspension, and scheduling issues..

  • Connection or network failures

  • Resource not found or unavailable

// Cause 4: Missing dependencies or resources
?>

How to Fix

Fix 1: Verify configuration and setup

<?php
// Check configuration values and ensure required setup
// Verify the package/library is properly configured
?>

Fix 2: Add proper error handling

<?php
try {
 // Use the package/library with proper error handling
} catch (\Exception $e) {
 error_log('Error: ' . $e->getMessage());
 // Handle gracefully
}
?>

Fix 3: Validate input and add checks

<?php
// Validate input before processing
// Check existence before accessing resources
// Use type declarations and strict comparisons
?>

Examples

<?php
// Common error handling pattern
try {
 $result = doSomething();
 echo $result;
} catch (\Exception $e) {
 error_log('Error: ' . $e->getMessage());
 http_response_code(500);
 echo json_encode(['error' => $e->getMessage()]);
}
?>

Related Errors

  • PHP Fatal Error — fatal error
  • PHP Warning — warning
  • PHP Notice — notice

item.title: [Solution] PHP Fiber Error: Cannot Suspend Fix
item.description:
PHP Fiber Error: Cannot Suspend Fix

A PHP Fiber error occurs when you try to suspend or resume a fiber in an invalid state. Fibers are lightweight cooperatively-scheduled threads introduced in PHP 8.1.

What This Error Means

PHP Fibers allow cooperative multitasking. The Fiber->suspend() and Fiber->resume() methods must follow strict rules: a fiber can only be suspended from within itself, and a fiber that has not been started or has already terminated cannot be resumed.

item.title: [Solution] PHP First Class Callable Error Fix
item.description:
PHP First Class Callable Error

Fix first class callable syntax errors in PHP 8.1+. Handle Closure::fromCallable migration and syntax..

?>

} catch (\Exception $e) {
 error_log('Error: ' . $e->getMessage());
 // Handle gracefully

Fix 3: Validate input and add checks

<?php

// Check existence before accessing resources
// Use type declarations and strict comparisons

Examples

// Common error handling pattern

?>

  • PHP Warning — warning
  • PHP Notice — notice

item.title: [Solution] PHP First Class Callable: Syntax Error Fix
item.description:
PHP First Class Callable: Syntax Error Fix

A PHP first class callable error occurs when you use incorrect syntax for creating first class callables. This feature was introduced in PHP 8.1.

What This Error Means

PHP 8.1 introduced first class callable syntax using Closure::fromCallable() or the short syntax strlen(...). This allows passing a function as a callable without wrapping it in a closure. Errors occur when the syntax is wrong or the function doesn’t exist.

item.title: [Solution] PHP Match Expression Error Fix
item.description:
PHP Match Expression Error

Fix match expression errors in PHP 8.0+. Handle exhaustive matching, type juggling, and return types..

What This Error Means

Common error scenarios include:

  • Connection or network failures
  • Invalid configuration or options
  • Resource not found or unavailable
  • Permission or access denied

Common Causes

<?php
// Cause 1: Incorrect configuration or missing setup
// Cause 2: Network or connection issues
// Cause 3: Invalid input or parameters
// Cause 4: Missing dependencies or resources
?>

How to Fix

Fix 1: Verify configuration and setup

<?php
// Check configuration values and ensure required setup
// Verify the package/library is properly configured
?>

Fix 2: Add proper error handling

<?php
try {
 // Use the package/library with proper error handling
} catch (\Exception $e) {
 error_log('Error: ' . $e->getMessage());
 // Handle gracefully
}
?>

Fix 3: Validate input and add checks

<?php
// Validate input before processing
// Check existence before accessing resources
// Use type declarations and strict comparisons
?>

Examples

<?php
// Common error handling pattern
try {
 $result = doSomething();
 echo $result;
} catch (\Exception $e) {
 error_log('Error: ' . $e->getMessage());
 http_response_code(500);
 echo json_encode(['error' => $e->getMessage()]);
}
?>

Related Errors

  • PHP Fatal Error — fatal error
  • PHP Warning — warning
  • PHP Notice — notice

item.title: [Solution] PHP Match: Unhandled Value Error Fix
item.description:
PHP Match: Unhandled Value Error Fix

A PHP match error occurs when the match expression encounters a value that is not handled by any arm and has no default branch. Unlike switch, match is strict about exhaustive handling.

What This Error Means

PHP 8.0 introduced the match expression as a strict alternative to switch. It uses strict comparison (===) and requires either all possible values to be covered or a default branch. If a value falls through without a match, PHP throws an UnhandledMatchError.

item.title: [Solution] PHP Named Argument Error Fix
item.description:
PHP Named Argument Error Fix

A PHP named argument error occurs when you use incorrect parameter names, mix positional and named arguments in the wrong order, or pass unexpected named arguments.

What This Error Means

PHP 8.0 introduced named arguments, allowing you to pass arguments by name instead of position. The parameter name must match exactly. Named arguments must come after positional arguments and cannot be skipped when mixed.

Common Causes

  • Misspelled parameter names
  • Passing named arguments to functions without named parameters
  • Mixing positional and named arguments incorrectly (positional after named)
  • Using reserved keyword as parameter name
  • Passing unknown named arguments

How to Fix

1. Use correct parameter names

<?php
function createUser(string $name, int $age, string $email): array {
 return compact('name', 'age', 'email');
}

// WRONG: Misspelled parameter name
// createUser($name: 'Alice', $age: 30, $emial: 'alice@example.com');

// CORRECT: Exact parameter name
createUser(name: 'Alice', age: 30, email: 'alice@example.com');
?>

2. Don’t mix positional and named incorrectly

<?php
function connect(string $host, int $port, string $db): void {
 echo "Connecting to $host:$port/$db\n";
}

// WRONG: Positional after named
// connect(host: 'localhost', 3306, 'mydb');

// CORRECT: Named arguments only, or positional only
connect(host: 'localhost', port: 3306, db: 'mydb');
// OR
connect('localhost', 3306, 'mydb');
?>

3. Only use valid parameter names

<?php
// WRONG: Using reserved word or wrong name
// Some functions have names that can't be used as named args

// CORRECT: Check the function signature first
// Look at: function foo(Type $param)
// Use: foo(param: value)
?>

4. Don’t pass unexpected named arguments

<?php
// WRONG: Extra named argument
// array_map(callback: 'strlen', array: $arr, extra: true);

// CORRECT: Only named arguments the function accepts
array_map('strlen', $arr);
?>

Related Errors

  • PHP Match error
  • PHP Parse error
  • PHP Deprecated function

  • PHP Notice — notice

item.title: [Solution] PHP Null Safe Operator Error Fix
item.description:
PHP Null Safe Operator Error

Fix null safe operator (?->) errors in PHP 8.0+. Handle null chaining and method calls..

What This Error Means

Common error scenarios include:

  • Connection or network failures
  • Invalid configuration or options
  • Resource not found or unavailable
  • Permission or access denied

Common Causes

<?php
// Cause 1: Incorrect configuration or missing setup
// Cause 2: Network or connection issues
// Cause 3: Invalid input or parameters
// Cause 4: Missing dependencies or resources
?>

How to Fix

Fix 1: Verify configuration and setup

<?php
// Check configuration values and ensure required setup
// Verify the package/library is properly configured
?>

Fix 2: Add proper error handling

<?php
try {
 // Use the package/library with proper error handling
} catch (\Exception $e) {
 error_log('Error: ' . $e->getMessage());
 // Handle gracefully
}
?>

Fix 3: Validate input and add checks

<?php
// Validate input before processing
// Check existence before accessing resources
// Use type declarations and strict comparisons
?>

Examples

<?php
// Common error handling pattern

 $result = doSomething();
 echo $result;
} catch (\Exception $e) {
 error_log('Error: ' . $e->getMessage());
 http_response_code(500);
 echo json_encode(['error' => $e->getMessage()]);
}
?>

Related Errors

  • PHP Fatal Error — fatal error
  • PHP Warning — warning
  • PHP Notice — notice

item.title: [Solution] PHP Null Safe Operator: On Null Error Fix
item.description:
PHP Null Safe Operator: On Null Error Fix

A PHP null safe error occurs when the null safe operator (?->) is used incorrectly or when a method call on null still triggers side effects.

What This Error Means

PHP 8.0 introduced the null safe operator (?->), which chains method calls that return null early. If the left side is null, the entire chain returns null without calling subsequent methods. Errors occur when developers misunderstand this behavior or use it in contexts where null is unexpected.

?>

Fix 2: Add proper error handling

 // Use the package/library with proper error handling

 // Handle gracefully

// Check existence before accessing resources
// Use type declarations and strict comparisons

Examples

// Common error handling pattern

 echo $result;
} catch (\Exception $e) {

 http_response_code(500);

// Common error handling pattern

  • PHP Warning — warning
  • PHP Notice — notice

item.title: [Solution] PHP Readonly: Modification Error Fix
item.description:
PHP Readonly: Modification Error Fix

A PHP readonly error occurs when you try to modify a readonly property after it has been initialized. Readonly properties can only be written to once.

What This Error Means

PHP 8.1 introduced readonly properties and PHP 8.2 introduced readonly classes. Once a readonly property is assigned a value in the constructor (or declared inline), any subsequent attempt to modify it throws an Error.

Common Causes

  • Trying to reassign a readonly property after construction
  • Unsetting a readonly property
  • Using clone on an object with readonly properties incorrectly
  • Attempting double initialization in constructor

How to Fix

1. Only assign readonly properties once

<?php
class User {
 public function __construct(
 public readonly string $name,
 public readonly string $email,
 ) {}
}

$user = new User('Alice', 'alice@example.com');

// WRONG: Reassigning readonly property
// $user->name = 'Bob'; // Error: cannot modify readonly property

// CORRECT: Create a new instance instead
$newUser = new User('Bob', 'bob@example.com');
?>

2. Handle readonly in clone scenarios

<?php
class Config {
 public function __construct(
 public readonly string $host,
 public readonly int $port,
 ) {}
}

$config = new Config('localhost', 3306);

// WRONG: Clone + modify readonly
// $newConfig = clone $config;
// $newConfig->port = 5432; // Error

// CORRECT: Use a factory method
function withPort(Config $config, int $port): Config {
 return new Config($config->host, $port);
}
$newConfig = withPort($config, 5432);
?>

3. Don’t double-initialize in constructor

<?php
class Service {
 public readonly string $name;

 public function __construct(string $name) {
 // WRONG: Double assignment
 // $this->name = $name;
 // $this->name = strtoupper($name); // Error

 // CORRECT: Single assignment
 $this->name = strtoupper($name);
 }
}
?>

4. Use readonly classes carefully

<?php
// PHP 8.2+: readonly class — all properties are readonly
readonly class Point {
 public function __construct(
 public float $x,
 public float $y,
 ) {}
}

$point = new Point(1.0, 2.0);
// $point->x = 3.0; // Error: cannot modify readonly property
?>

Related Errors

  • PHP Parse error
  • PHP Dynamic property error
  • PHP Deprecated function

item.title: [Solution] PHP Trailing Comma Error Fix
item.description:
PHP Trailing Comma Error

Fix trailing comma errors in function calls. Handle PHP 7.3+ trailing comma support and syntax..

What This Error Means

Common error scenarios include:

  • Connection or network failures
  • Invalid configuration or options
  • Resource not found or unavailable
  • Permission or access denied

Common Causes

<?php
// Cause 1: Incorrect configuration or missing setup
// Cause 2: Network or connection issues
// Cause 3: Invalid input or parameters
// Cause 4: Missing dependencies or resources
?>

How to Fix

Fix 1: Verify configuration and setup

<?php
// Check configuration values and ensure required setup
// Verify the package/library is properly configured
?>

Fix 2: Add proper error handling

<?php
try {
 // Use the package/library with proper error handling
} catch (\Exception $e) {
 error_log('Error: ' . $e->getMessage());
 // Handle gracefully
}
?>

Fix 3: Validate input and add checks

<?php
// Validate input before processing
// Check existence before accessing resources
// Use type declarations and strict comparisons
?>

Examples

<?php
// Common error handling pattern
try {
 $result = doSomething();
 echo $result;
} catch (\Exception $e) {
 error_log('Error: ' . $e->getMessage());
 http_response_code(500);
 echo json_encode(['error' => $e->getMessage()]);
}
?>

Related Errors

  • PHP Fatal Error — fatal error
  • PHP Warning — warning
  • PHP Notice — notice

item.title: [Solution] PHP Union Type Error Fix
item.description:
PHP Union Type Error

Fix union type errors in PHP 8.0+. Handle type declarations, type checking, and compatibility..

What This Error Means

Common error scenarios include:

  • Connection or network failures
  • Invalid configuration or options
  • Resource not found or unavailable
  • Permission or access denied

Common Causes

<?php
// Cause 1: Incorrect configuration or missing setup
// Cause 2: Network or connection issues
// Cause 3: Invalid input or parameters
// Cause 4: Missing dependencies or resources
?>

How to Fix

Fix 1: Verify configuration and setup

<?php
// Check configuration values and ensure required setup
// Verify the package/library is properly configured
?>

Fix 2: Add proper error handling

<?php
try {
 // Use the package/library with proper error handling
} catch (\Exception $e) {
 error_log('Error: ' . $e->getMessage());
 // Handle gracefully
}
?>

Fix 3: Validate input and add checks

<?php
// Validate input before processing
// Check existence before accessing resources
// Use type declarations and strict comparisons
?>

Examples

<?php
// Common error handling pattern
try {
 $result = doSomething();
 echo $result;
} catch (\Exception $e) {
 error_log('Error: ' . $e->getMessage());
 http_response_code(500);
 echo json_encode(['error' => $e->getMessage()]);
}
?>

Related Errors

  • PHP Fatal Error — fatal error
  • PHP Warning — warning
  • PHP Notice — notice

item.title: Class 'X' not found
item.description:
This error occurs when PHP’s autoloader (managed by Composer) cannot find the class file for the class you are referencing. It is common after adding new classes, changing namespaces, or forgetting to run composer dump-autoload.

Common Causes

  • The class was recently created or renamed but composer dump-autoload was not run
  • The class namespace does not match the directory structure
  • The class is in a package that has not been required via Composer
  • Typo in the class name

How to Fix

Regenerate the Composer autoload map:

item.title: Method X does not exist on instance
item.description:
This error occurs when you call a method on an object that does not have that method defined. In Laravel projects it frequently happens with Eloquent models when accessing relationships via method syntax without defining them, or when using a Facade that resolves to an unexpected class.

Common Causes

  • Calling a relationship method (e.g. $user->posts()) without defining it on the model
  • Using an outdated package version where a method was removed or renamed
  • Calling a method on a Facade that resolves to a different underlying class than expected
  • Typo in the method name

How to Fix

Define the relationship method on the Eloquent model:

item.title: [Solution] PHP session_register() Deprecated — Use $_SESSION Superglobal
item.description:
[Solution] PHP session_register() Deprecated — Use $_SESSION Superglobal

The session_register() function was deprecated in PHP 4.2 and removed in PHP 5.4. It registered a variable as a session variable, automatically making it available in the $_SESSION superglobal array. The modern approach is to work directly with the $_SESSION superglobal, which gives you full control over session data and is consistent with how all other superglobals ($_GET, $_POST, $_REQUEST) work in PHP.
